Jquiver provides with a database level implementation of application configurations, wherein you can manage all you environment and system related properties. Property Master table contains below configurations :
| Property Name | Usage | | -------- | -------- | | authentication-type | Authentication type - default in memory authentication | | enable-user-management |By default user management will be disabled | | max-version-count | Maximum limit to persist versioning data | | regexPattern | Regex Pattern to validate password | | user-profile-form-details | Dynamic Form for add edit user details | | user-profile-template-details | Custom template for user profile listing page | | user-profile-form-name | Dynamic Form for user profile | | enable-google-analytics | To add google analytics on each screen | | google-analytics-key | Google analytics key,that needs to be passed to GA | | jws-date-format | Date Format to be used at various levels such as on, UI layer, Java Layer, and Database.Stored in JSON format | | version | JQuiver Application Version | | template-storage-path | Path at which the template will be stored during local development. | | base-url | To fetch the base url of your application. | | maxFailedCount | After 5 attempts of unsuccessful login, the users account will be locked. | | jwsSecretKey | Default secret key to be used in appropriate requirements. | | JwkProvider | An interface that provides access to JSON Web Keys (JWKs) | | last-deployed-version | Depicts the last deployed version. | | passwordExpiry | Sets the password expiry limit. | | isActivityLogEnabled | By default ActivityLog will be disabled. | | last-deployed-date | Date on which the last version was deployed. | adminEmailId | Email Id with which you sign into the application. | | file-copy-path | Path where the files would be stored. | | otp_expiry_time | Time limit set for the OTP to expire. | | default-redirect-url | Default redirect URL set. | | auto-delete-notification | By default the notifications are automatically deleted. | | mail-configuration | Stores the configuration of the mail server . | | clusterActiveTime | Sets the active cluster time . | | scheduler-url | Sets the default scheduler url. | | daily-notification-count | Sets the daily notification count. | | notification-validity | Sets the validity of the notification. | | file-upload-location | Sets the file upload location. | | isStaticImportDone | Check whether database is imported for the first time. | | acl-jws | Default role for jws. | | enableSecuredAuthentication | Enables secured authentication and security validation mechanisms. | | enableCSP | Enables Content Security Policy (CSP) security headers. | | cspHeader | Configures CSP header directives for browser-side script execution control. | | session-timeout | Configures user session timeout duration. | | enableOtpAuthentication | Enables OTP-based authentication mechanism. | | account-lock-duration | Defines account lock duration after failed login attempts. |
You can run JQuiver Application in Production Mode. (prod)
<u>**Mail Configuration**</u>
JQuiver has in-built classes and methods to configure mails. To enable mail configuration goto "Mail Configuration" page, by clicking on the icon.
![](/cf/files/36675e75-029e-4d46-966f-f5060ae3099a)
Here provide the details like, SMTP Host, SMTP Port, Security Protocol, as per your mail server by default the following values are set as shown in the image.
![](/cf/files/22f806b9-3ab1-45db-90de-575651d64e0a) ![](/cf/files/4abc8a9f-bf90-4008-a517-30c06ec6e4e6)
This page also allows you to set the "From" and "Reply To" mail ids and also to attach files as shown in the picture above.
By clicking on the save configuration, the email configuration property gets saved in the property_master table. ![](/cf/files/37bb5551-34fb-417f-a7f4-c59a6645ae00)
There is a provision to send test mail by clicking on the button. ![](/cf/files/a410b60d-4687-4c18-b7fa-9e744acfffaa)
There is also provision for attaching footer and files to mail as shown below. ![](/cf/files/740c4dd8-4e35-4691-bc53-da82942b1e0b)
<u>**Enabling Google-Analytics:**</u>
Application configuration also provides you with enabling Google Analytics, Create a Google Analytics Key and add it in **google-analytics-key** ![](/cf/files/fbeb2e30-eee3-42db-8048-ead0a5a60167) and also you need to set the property of **enable-google-analytics** to **true** ![](/cf/files/fc650462-72af-4a16-a074-2829d6d1c84f)

The Workflow Definition module enables administrators to design and manage business workflows within the JQuiver platform.Workflows define the sequence of tasks, approvals, and decision points required to complete a business process.JQuiver workflows are created using BPMN (Business Process Model and Notation) and are stored in BPMN XML format, which is executed by the workflow engine.
<u>**Follow the steps below to create a new workflow definition.**</u>
**Step 1: **Navigate to the Workflow Definition module from the JQuiver home page and Click Create New to create a new workflow.
![](/cf/files/40b9f222-f247-4e1d-a784-58b34ddf770d)
**Step 2: **Configure workflow definition.
Enter a unique Definition Name to identify the workflow definition.
After specifying the Definition Name, the workflow definition can be configured using one of the following methods.
**Upload Existing BPMN File** If the workflow definition is already available as a BPMN file, it can be uploaded directly using the Upload File option. Upon successful upload, the corresponding BPMN XML content will be automatically populated in the BPMN XML Editor for validation or modification.
**Generate BPMN Workflow** If a BPMN file is not available, click BPMN Generator to launch the workflow designer. Use the designer interface to construct the workflow by configuring the required BPMN elements such as Start Events, User Tasks, Gateways and End Events. After completing the workflow design, export the generated BPMN XML file and upload it using the Upload File option. Once uploaded, the XML structure will be rendered in the BPMN XML Editor for further review or updates.
![](/cf/files/2f0225bc-369c-4bfa-98ef-e10a5fc77566)
**Step 3: **Click Save & Return to save the workflow definition.
![](/cf/files/c1d1b663-374b-4288-aef7-925e457dc64b)
**Step 4: **Upon successful save, the workflow definition will be listed in the Workflow Definition listing page.

This module is a programmatic solution designed to manage file-related operations like upload, retrieval, modification, and deletion, tailored to fit the specific needs of an application.
Unlike generic or third-party storage systems, this module is built in-house, giving full control over how files are handled.
**Note:** Custom File Storage provision is given in the Master Generator Module as shown in the image below.
![](/cf/files/8adc713b-8de3-4240-aaa0-fd05d48d7650)
🔹 <u>**Key Benefits**</u> ✅ **Custom Logic Support:** Designed with business-specific rules like naming conventions, directory structures, and metadata.
✅ **Security:** Allows implementation of custom encryption, access control, and authentication mechanisms.
✅ **Integration-Friendly:** Seamlessly integrates with internal systems such as database, user services, or business workflows.
✅ **Third-Party Independence:** Eliminates reliance on external file libraries or storage services, reducing security and compatibility risks.
✅ **Structured File Access:** Abstracts file operations into clean APIs that developers can reuse easily across the application.
🔹 <u>**Common Features to Implement**</u>
<u>**File Upload**</u>
* Accept file via input stream or multipart request.
* Validate file type, size, and structure.
* Save file using custom naming and directory logic.
<u>**File Retrieval**</u>
* Provide file by its unique identifier or name.
* Optionally return metadata (e.g., uploaded time, user).
<u>**File Update / Replace**</u>
* Replace an existing file while maintaining version control or audit log.
<u>** File Delete**</u>
* Securely delete a file from the storage and optionally archive or log the event.
<u>**Metadata Handling**</u>
* Save and retrieve metadata such as file owner, access permissions, and description.
<u>**Custom Directory Management**</u>
Define directory structure based on user, module, date, etc.
<u>**Encryption (Optional)**</u>
Encrypt files at rest or during transmission using AES, RSA, etc.
<u>**Access Control**</u>
Implement file-level or folder-level permissions based on user roles.
🔹<u> **Developer Best Practices**</u>
Store files outside the web root to prevent direct access.
Use UUIDs or hash-based names to prevent file name conflicts.
Keep a file log or database table to track file uploads and metadata.
Sanitize file names and validate file content to prevent malicious uploads.
Separate concerns: keep file logic inside the storage module, not scattered across the codebase.
🔹 <u>**Example Use Cases**</u>
User profile picture storage with per-user folders.
Uploading and encrypting confidential reports with role-based access.
Storing help manual images and documents in structured paths.
Handling large video/audio files with streaming support.

Universal Regex refers to a generalized, reusable set of regular expressions that can validate multiple data types across different systems or databases in a consistent way.
**It's especially useful when:**
* Reading data from different databases with varying types (e.g., INT, VARCHAR, DECIMAL, etc.)
* Validating dynamic form inputs
* Enforcing constraints on JSON or API payloads
🎯 <u>**Objectives of Universal Regex**</u>
✅ Cross-database compatibility
✅ Type-safe input validation
✅ Prevent invalid data entry
✅ Dynamic generation based on metadata (e.g., JDBC metadata)
**How can I use Universal Regex for data validation?**
Use Universal Regex to validate different types of input data in a consistent and reusable way across your application. It allows you to dynamically generate validation patterns based on data types such as integers, text, or decimals, making it especially useful when working with multiple databases or dynamic forms. By leveraging metadata (e.g., from JDBC), you can automatically create type-specific regex patterns that enforce constraints like length, precision, and format. This helps prevent invalid data entry, ensures type safety, and maintains consistency across APIs, forms, and database operations.

The View REST API module allows users to inspect and review all REST APIs configured within the system. This module provides information such as the API endpoint URL, HTTP method, description, response headers, and role-based access permissions associated with each API.
<u>**Follow the steps below to view REST APIs.**</u>
**Step 1: **Access the View REST API module from the REST API module on the JQuiver Home page.
![](/cf/files/406a6c70-be22-4ac9-abb1-d53708377dc7)
The system displays all available REST API endpoints along with their complete endpoint URLs.
**Step 2: **Use the Expand All option to display all REST API methods simultaneously instead of expanding each API individually.
When expanded, the system displays additional API metadata including:
* HTTP Produce Type * REST Method Name * API Description * Response Headers * Authorized Roles
![](/cf/files/fdbe16be-e8e5-4461-b715-aa06134ea937)
**Step 3: **Apply sorting and filtering options to locate specific APIs within the list.
Users can sort the APIs using the available criteria:
* By Name * By Type * By URL
![](/cf/files/886a85ea-7b4e-4a16-8370-c9802a7ffb20)
**Step 4: **Users can export the REST API list for documentation or reference purposes.
* Select a single API, multiple APIs or all APIs from the list. * Export the selected APIs as a PDF document.
![](/cf/files/502af235-a89a-4f98-9462-05d905fcde1a) ![](/cf/files/18fed455-c5d7-49ca-b53d-03411849e899)

The API Clients module enables users to create and manage client credentials used for secure communication with REST APIs. This module maintains configuration details such as the Client Name, Client Key, Encryption Algorithm, and other security parameters required for API authentication and encryption.
<u>**Follow the steps below to configure a new API Client.**</u>
**Step 1: **Access the API Clients module from the REST API module on the JQuiver Home page.
![](/cf/files/fb2301ea-3359-481b-ab46-0de772d88cb0)
**Step 2: **Click Create New to create a new API client configuration.
![](/cf/files/7f47fbca-4544-4dcd-8b10-e6e48533ec42)
**Step 3: **Provide the API client configuration details. * Specify the Client Name to uniquely identify the API client application. * Select the required Encryption Algorithm used for secure communication. Available options include AES, DES, and RSA. * Select the appropriate Encryption Mode for the chosen algorithm. Available options include CBC and ECB. * Specify the Padding Method used during the encryption process. * Enter the Key Length (in bits) to define the size of the encryption key.
The system automatically generates the Client Key, while the Client Secret must be entered manually by the user for API authentication.
![](/cf/files/65cc2f8e-1c75-4649-b143-0628b27fc1e7)
**Step 4: **Validate encryption and decryption. Users can verify the encryption configuration using the Test Input section.
![](/cf/files/65cc2f8e-1c75-4649-b143-0628b27fc1e7)
* Test Input – Enter sample text to validate encryption. * Encrypt – Converts the input text into encrypted format. * Decrypt – Converts the encrypted text back to the original format. * Test Output – Displays the result after encryption or decryption.
**Step 5: **After entering the required configuration details, click Save & Return to store the API client configuration.
![](/cf/files/65cc2f8e-1c75-4649-b143-0628b27fc1e7)

Custom Utility in JQuiver provides a flexible space for developers to write project-specific enhancements without altering the core system functionality. It includes two key resources:
**<u>custom-js:</u>** This serves as a custom script area specifically designed for the implementer (developer integrating or customizing the system) to add project-specific behavior. This is present in Templating Module.
**<u>Key Features:</u>**
* Designated for Custom Logic
* This file is intentionally left minimal.
* Use it to add or override JavaScript logic without modifying core scripts.
**<u>jQuery Ready Handler:</u>**
* $(function() {...}); ensures all code inside executes after the DOM is fully loaded.
* Safe to place DOM-dependent code here.
* Commented Console Log
* The line //console.log("ready!"); is a placeholder.
* Uncomment it for testing if the script is successfully loaded and the DOM is ready.
**<u>custom-style-css:</u>** This stylesheet is designated for custom visual styling applied by the implementer. It allows developers to override default styles or add new UI elements without touching core CSS files, ensuring easier upgrades and maintenance.
**<u>Key Features:</u>**
* All class names are organized by functional groups.
* Empty class blocks are placeholders; implementer should populate with desired styles.
* Use these classes to maintain consistency, modularity, and responsiveness across pages.

Monaco Smart Suggestions is to provide fast, intelligent, and context-aware code assistance that helps users write expressions, scripts, queries, or programming logic quickly, accurately, and efficiently.
It reduces manual typing, minimizes syntax errors, improves development speed, and enhances the overall coding experience through intelligent autocomplete recommendations.
**<u>Purpose Of Monaco Smart Suggestions:</u>**
Monaco Smart Suggestions are designed to:
* Improve coding speed. * Reduce typing effort. * Minimize syntax mistakes. * Help users discover available functions and expressions. * Provide real-time assistance while writing logic. * Improve user productivity and accuracy. * Enable easier script and expression building.
**<u>Features:</u>**
**1. Real-Time Autocomplete**
Automatically shows suggestions while the user types without needing manual trigger.
**2. Error Reduction**
Helps avoid syntax errors and incorrect usage of variables or functions.
**3. Syntax-Aware Assistance**
Provides only valid syntax-based suggestions such as directives, variables, functions, and expressions.
<u>Monaco Smart Suggestions Are Used In:</u>
**1.Templates** In the template editor, Monaco Smart Suggestions are triggered when the user types '@'. A dropdown list of available variables and context objects is displayed. Supports FTL (FreeMarker Template Language) expressions.
**<u>Example:</u>** ``` ${loggedInUserName!''} ```
**2.FormBuilder:**
Monaco Smart Suggestions in a Form Builder provide real-time, context-aware autocomplete suggestions in Script, HTML Script, and Query Editor. When user type @ it will provide the relevant suggestions variables, objects, and expressions to easily build scripts, dynamic content, and queries.
Monaco Smart Suggestions are available in the following editors within the Form Builder:
* Script Editor * HTML Script Editor * Query Editor
**<u>Example:</u>**
requestDetails[""]
**3.RestAPI**
Monaco Smart Suggestions in the REST API Service Logic editor provide real-time, context-aware autocomplete support for building API logic efficiently.
Suggestions are triggered as the user types # symbol displaying a list of available functions, variables, and objects.
It supports both:
* JavaScript-based Service Logic * FTL (FreeMarker) Template-based responses
These suggestions help users easily access request data, execute APIs, process responses, and build dynamic logic without memorizing platform-specific syntax.
**1.PlatformType-Javascript:**
In RestAPI, when the platform type is set to JavaScript, Monaco Smart Suggestions provide real-time, context-aware autocomplete support for writing service logic. When user types # symbol, then context aware autocomplete suggestions dropdown is displayed that includes variables and objects. The expressions support the javasript language syntax.
The suggestions that supports for javascript suggestions in restapi is:
* #httpRequestObject * #jq_callStoredProcedure * #header * #requestDetails
**<u>Example:</u>** ``` requestDetails["loggedInUserName"] ``` **2.Platform Type - FTL:**
If the platform type is set to FTL (FreeMarker Template Language), Monaco Smart Suggestions provide real-time, context-aware autocomplete support for writing template-based API responses.
When user types # symbol, then context aware autocomplete suggestions dropdown is displayed that includes variables and objects. The expressions support the javasript language syntax.
The suggestions that supports for Free marker Language(FTL) suggestions in restapi is:
* #httpRequestObject * #templateWithParams * #loggedInUserName * #loggedInUserId
**<u>Example:</u>** ``` ${messageSource.getMessage('your.key') !''} ```
**4.Autocomplete**
Monaco Smart Suggestions provides instant dropdown suggestions while the user types @ symbol, helping users quickly select valid syntax elements such as variables objects, and expressions. It provides the expressions in ftl syntax.
**<u>Example:</u>** ``` ${loggedInUserName!''} ```

The Script Library feature allows developers to define and reuse JavaScript functions across REST APIs using the Nashorn Script Engine. This promotes code reuse, centralized script management, and easier maintenance of custom business logic.
🧠** <u>Key Features:</u>** ✅ **Global Script Library**
* A centralized script repository.
* Functions defined here are automatically available to all REST APIs.
✅ **API-Specific Script Library**
* REST APIs can have custom script blocks.
* These scripts are executed in addition to the global library.
🚀 **Nashorn Engine Support**
* Java's Nashorn engine executes the JavaScript code.
* Can access Java classes and perform server-side logic securely.
* Script Library feature is also available in Form Builder and File Bins.

JavaMelody is an open-source real-time monitoring tool for Java web applications. It helps developers and system administrators observe the performance, usage, and behavior of an application in production or testing environments.
🎯 <u>**Key Features**</u>
📊 Monitors HTTP requests, SQL queries, Spring beans, memory usage, GC, CPU load, etc.
🛠 Integrates easily with Spring Boot, Java EE, JSF, Struts, GWT, etc.
🧠 Helps identify performance bottlenecks and memory issues.
📁 Exports reports in HTML, PDF, CSV, etc.
🔐 Supports security features like IP filtering and user authentication.
⚙️ <u>**YAML Configuration Overview**</u>
```# Uncomment below code if JavaMelody configuration has to be set``` javamelody: enabled: true # Enables JavaMelody monitoring spring-monitoring-enabled: true # Enables monitoring of Spring beans init-parameters: url-exclude-pattern: (/webjars/.*|/css/.*|/images/.*|/fonts/.*|/js/.*) monitoring-path: /monitoring
🔧 <u>**Configuration Explained:**</u>
| Parameter | Description | | --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | `enabled: true` | Turns on JavaMelody for the application. | | `spring-monitoring-enabled` | Enables monitoring of Spring services and beans (method execution times, etc.). | | `url-exclude-pattern` | Excludes static resources (like JS, CSS, images) from monitoring to reduce overhead and noise in metrics. | | `monitoring-path` | Sets the custom URL path where the monitoring console is accessible (e.g., `/monitoring`). Default is also `/monitoring`. |
🖥️ <u>**Accessing the Monitoring Console**</u>
Once enabled and the application is running, you can access the monitoring dashboard at:
```http://<host>:<port>/<context-path>/monitoring```
**Example:**
http://localhost:8080/myapp/monitoring
**This dashboard provides real-time graphs and tables for:**
* Active HTTP sessions
* Response times
* Database query statistics
* Heap and non-heap memory usage
* Thread and error metrics
📝 <u>**Additional Notes**</u>
* Lightweight: JavaMelody has minimal performance overhead.
* Works with embedded servers like Tomcat, Jetty, Undertow (in Spring Boot).
* Does not require agents or JVM arguments—just a few dependencies and config.

This is a security feature that helps prevent cross-site scripting (XSS), data injection attacks, and other code-injection vulnerabilities by restricting the sources from which content can be loaded on a web page.
🔹 <u>**Configuration Details**</u>
✅** isCSPEnable: "true"**
* Enables Content Security Policy for the application.
* Once enabled, CSP headers will be added to the HTTP response to control which scripts are allowed to run.
✅** cspHeader: "script-src 'self' 'nonce-%nonce%';"**
* Restricts the execution of JavaScript only to trusted sources.
**Breakdown of the directive:**
* **script-src:** Specifies allowed script sources.
* **'self':** Only allows scripts that originate from the same origin (i.e., the same domain as the app).
* '**nonce-%nonce%':** Allows scripts with a dynamically generated nonce (number used once) attribute.
* **%nonce%** is a placeholder that should be replaced with a secure random value at runtime.
* Only scripts with matching nonce values will be allowed to run.
**Example:**
```<script nonce="a1b2c3d4">alert('This is allowed');</script>```
🛡️ <u>**Benefits of Using CSP with Nonce**</u>
* Prevents unauthorized inline scripts from executing.
* Protects against XSS attacks even if malicious script tags are injected.
* Encourages a safer coding practice by avoiding eval() or ```<script>``` without a valid nonce.
⚙️ <u>**Developer Guidelines**</u>
* Ensure the server generates a unique nonce per request and injects it.
* In the CSP response header.
* In each ```<script>``` tag in the HTML.
* Avoid using inline event handlers like onclick="..." unless nonce is added properly.
* Avoid using 'unsafe-inline' unless absolutely necessary (not recommended for production).
🚫 <u>**What Happens If You Don't Comply?**</u>
* Any ```<script>``` that doesn't have the required nonce or doesn't come from 'self' will be blocked by the browser.
* Helps you catch and fix unsafe scripting practices during development.
📝<u>** Sample HTTP Response Header**</u>
```Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self' 'nonce-a1b2c3d4';```
🔄 %nonce% must be replaced dynamically by your backend system (e.g., Java, Node.js, etc.) for each request.</script>
<u>**How can I handle CSP Issue?**</u>
When **CSP Enabled = true,** the browser strictly enforces the configured Content Security Policy (CSP) directives and rejects the execution of non-compliant inline JavaScript resources. To satisfy CSP validation requirements, all inline ```<script>``` elements must include a server-generated nonce attribute that is cryptographically unique for every HTTP request lifecycle.
**Example :** ```<script ${scriptNonce}>``` // JavaScript logic ```</script>```
The ${scriptNonce} placeholder denotes the dynamically injected nonce token generated by the backend security layer during request processing. The generated nonce value must correspond exactly with the nonce declared within the Content-Security-Policy response header.

It is an intuitive tool in JQuiver that allows users to visually organize and manage items—such as router menus—through a simple drag-and-drop interface. It eliminates the need for manual configuration by providing a user-friendly way to structure hierarchical data like menus, categories, or components.
**JQuiver** provides a **Visual Drag-and-Drop (DND) Organiser** to easily manage and rearrange router menus without editing code manually. This tool helps administrators and developers visually structure the navigation layout of the application.
🔍 <u>**Key Features:**</u>
* **Drag-and-Drop Interface:** Simply drag menu items to rearrange their order or nest them under parent menus.
* **Hierarchy Support:** Supports multi-level nesting for submenu structures.
* **Live Preview:** Instantly view how the router menus will appear in the application.
* **User-Friendly UI:** No coding required – ideal for non-developers and content admins.
🧩<u>** Common Use Cases:**</u>
* Reordering top-level menus
* Creating nested submenus under specific modules
* Hiding or showing certain menus dynamically
* Structuring menus for user roles or access levels
⚙️ <u>**How It Works:**</u>
* Navigate to the Router Menu Organiser screen under the Admin or Configuration section.
* Use drag-and-drop to change the position of any menu item.
* Save the new structure to persist the changes.
* The updated menu order will reflect across the application UI based on user access rights.
📌 **Note:** Changes are saved to the routing configuration stored in the database.

The Scheduler module enables automated execution of REST APIs at predefined time intervals using Cron Expressions. Schedulers invoke the configured Target API automatically based on the defined scheduling configuration.
<u>**Follow the steps below to configure a new Scheduler.**</u>
**Step 1: **Access the Scheduler module from the REST API module on the JQuiver Home page.
![](/cf/files/aa679f61-af8e-4e4a-8bc0-b545de022de5)
**Step 2: **Click Create New to create a new scheduler configuration.
![](/cf/files/76c81654-0396-4fdf-9559-941f7d43d0ea)
**Step 3: **Configure the scheduler parameters. * Specify a Scheduler Name to uniquely identify the scheduler configuration within the system. * Select the Target API from the available options in the dropdown. The selected API will be invoked automatically based on the configured schedule. * Define the Cron Expression to determine the execution schedule. The expression can be entered manually or generated using the Cron Builder. * Use the Status Toggle to activate or deactivate the scheduler configuration.
![](/cf/files/1b6c3b29-88c5-4cb2-bfee-a0c639c4c304)
**NOTE: ** The Cron Builder provides a graphical interface to construct cron expressions for defining scheduler execution intervals. The generated cron expression is automatically populated in the Resulting Cron Expression field.
![](/cf/files/fc3773c7-1ece-4b87-8a4f-3bb875e54b34)
**Step 4: **After entering the required configuration details, click Save to store the scheduler configuration.
![](/cf/files/0d9fef2c-9e7a-41ea-88bb-d61e74d61de9)
**Step 5: **Manage scheduler actions. * Edit – Modify or update the scheduler configuration. * Delete – Remove the scheduler configuration from the system. * Execute Now – Manually trigger the scheduler to execute the configured API immediately.
![](/cf/files/02714c7b-2b5c-4f85-a68b-7c01af92c6a2)

A Success Event Listener is a callback function or handler that gets triggered automatically after a specific operation completes successfully. In JQuiver (and Form.io or similar platforms), it is commonly used to execute custom logic—such as showing a confirmation message, redirecting the user, or triggering another action—after a form is successfully submitted or data is saved.
**Example:**
<u>**LogoutSuccessEventListener:**</u>
The LogoutSuccessEventListener is a callback interface designed to provide a hook for custom logic when a user successfully logs out of the system.
<u>**Purpose:**</u>
* Enables developers to perform custom actions after logout, such as:
* Logging user activity.
* Auditing or tracking session termination.
* Cleaning up user-specific resources (e.g., caches, temp files).
* Sending logout notifications.
<u>**LoginSuccessEventListener:**</u>
This is a callback interface designed to allow developers to hook into the system after a successful user login. It provides a structured way to execute custom logic when a user logs in.
<u>**Purpose:**</u>
* Enables post-login processing such as:
* Logging login activity
* Initializing user context or preferences
* Triggering welcome messages or onboarding logic
* Auditing login events

Caching is a technique used to store frequently accessed data in memory to reduce response times and improve overall application performance. Instead of repeatedly fetching the same data from a database or external service, the application retrieves it from a faster, in-memory store (like Redis), reducing load and latency. JQuiver supports Redis-based caching using the Redisson client. This helps improve application performance by storing frequently accessed data in memory.
✅ <u>**Steps to Enable Redis Caching:**</u>
**1. Install and Start Redis Server**
* Redis must be running for Redisson to work. * Install Redis (if not already installed):
```sudo apt install redis-server # Linux brew install redis # macOS```
* **Start the Redis server:**
redis-server
**2. Update redisson.yaml Configuration File:**
This file contains Redisson's settings to connect to the Redis server.
📍** File location:** Usually placed in the resources folder or an external config path.
<u>**Sample redisson.yaml:**</u>
| **Parameter** | **Value** | **Description** | | --------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------- | | `singleServerConfig.address` | `redis://localhost:6379` | Redis server address and port | | `singleServerConfig.clientName` | `JQuiverApplication` | Name of the Redis client | | `singleServerConfig.password` | `null` | Password (if Redis auth is enabled) | | `singleServerConfig.connectionPoolSize` | `64` | Max total Redis connections in the pool | | `singleServerConfig.connectionMinimumIdleSize` | `16` | Minimum idle connections | | `singleServerConfig.subscriptionConnectionPoolSize` | `32` | Pool size for Redis pub/sub | | `singleServerConfig.idleConnectionTimeout` | `10000` | Idle connection timeout (ms) | | `singleServerConfig.connectTimeout` | `10000` | Timeout for initial connection (ms) | | `singleServerConfig.timeout` | `5000` | Command execution timeout (ms) | | `singleServerConfig.retryAttempts` | `5` | Number of retry attempts on failure | | `singleServerConfig.retryInterval` | `1000` | Interval between retry attempts (ms) | | `singleServerConfig.dnsMonitoringInterval` | `5000` | Interval for DNS monitoring (ms) | | `threads` | `8` | Number of threads used by Redisson worker threads | | `nettyThreads` | `16` | Netty I/O thread count | | `transportMode` | `NIO` | Network transport mode (`NIO`, `EPOLL`, or `KQUEUE`) |
**3. Enable Redis Caching in application.yml**
**Uncomment and update the Redis configuration block in application.yml.**
```redis: config: #Provide the path to redisson.yaml path: redisson.yaml # Use absolute path if stored externally cache: enabled: true # Change this to true to enable caching ttl: 1800 # Time-to-live (in seconds) idle: 1800 # Idle time before cache entry is evicted```
🔧 **Note:**
* If the redisson.yaml file is outside resources, use file:/absolute/path/to/redisson.yaml
* TTL (Time-to-Live) and Idle values control how long cache entries are retained.
**4. Restart the Application**
After updating the config files, restart the JQuiver application for changes to take effect.

The Additional Datasource feature allows JQuiver to connect to external databases beyond the default system repository. This enables REST APIs and application services to perform CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations across distributed data environments.
The platform currently supports the following RDBMS:
1.MariaDB 2.MySQL 3.PostgreSQL 4.Microsoft SQL Server 5.Oracle
<u>**Follow the steps below to configure a new Additional DataSource.**</u>
**Step 1: ** Navigate to the REST API module from the JQuiver Home and select the Additional Datasource module.
![](/cf/files/24f7ba0a-8a3c-4df6-8773-cac4bb465085)
**Step 2: ** Click Add Datasource to create a new datasource entry and configure the connection details.
![](/cf/files/f162c5d7-fb62-4840-be57-c64b2f4193d5) ![](/cf/files/de353224-5bd3-4822-ad43-6eccc0054cec)
Fill in the required datasource configuration details. * Specify a Datasource Name to uniquely identify the datasource within the application. * Select the required Database Type from the available options in the dropdown. * Provide the Host Name / Server where the database instance is hosted. * Enter the Port Number used to establish the database connection. * Specify the Database Name of the target database schema. * Provide the Username used for database authentication. * Enter the corresponding Password associated with the database user credentials.
**Step 3: ** Click Test Connection to verify the connectivity between the application and the configured database server.
![](/cf/files/d6b96e30-9386-461e-8e83-41048e2460db)
**Step 4: ** Click Save & Return to persist the datasource configuration and return to the datasource listing page.
![](/cf/files/d6b96e30-9386-461e-8e83-41048e2460db)
**Step 5: ** Manage datasource actions. * Edit – Update or modify the existing datasource configuration. * Copy – Copy the Datasource ID for use in APIs or system configurations. * Delete – Remove the datasource configuration from the system.
![](/cf/files/b8c5707d-4e52-4a14-be13-aea7d871135a)
**Step 6: **Using the Datasource ID in Queries
After creating the datasource, the generated Datasource ID can be used to execute queries on the configured external database. JQuiver provides two methods to utilize the datasource.
**Using Datasource ID in Script** The Datasource ID can be referenced directly in server-side scripts to execute queries against the external database.
![](/cf/files/11e78201-7870-443b-af8d-967e3fc89163)
**Using Datasource in Query Configuration**
Queries can also be executed by selecting the configured datasource from the Datasource dropdown in the Query Configuration section. The selected datasource determines the database connection used to run the query.
![](/cf/files/0a3ee020-753b-42d9-a0f6-10b9fee595ce) ![](/cf/files/097e1378-7d42-44de-839d-67d47d5957ae)

It is a platform that allows you to package applications and their dependencies into a container — a lightweight, portable, and self-sufficient unit that can run anywhere.
**<u>1. Dockerfile Overview</u>** The Dockerfile defines how the application image is built and what it does when started.
🔧<u>** Key Instructions:**</u>
| Instruction | Purpose | | ------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | | `FROM openjdk:22` | Uses OpenJDK 22 as the base image | | `COPY target/${application jar name}.jar app.jar` | Copies the compiled application JAR | | `COPY target/application.yml` | Adds internal `application.yml` | | `COPY target/logback.xml` | Adds internal `logback.xml` | | `EXPOSE ${APPLICATION_PORT}` | Exposes the application port | | `ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "/app.jar"]` | Default command to start the app |
📌 Optional Entrypoints are included for using external config files. You can uncomment as needed.
🧩** <u>2. Docker Compose Overview:</u>** This section explains how to configure and use the docker-compose.yml to run the Java application (api_service) and optionally connect to a MariaDB container.
📁 **<u>Docker Compose Services</u>**
🔹 **<u>Application Service (api_service)</u>**
| Key | Description | | --------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | `build: .` | Builds the Docker image from the current directory using the Dockerfile | | `restart: always` | Automatically restarts the container if it crashes | | `ports:` | Maps host port to container port using environment variable `APPLICATION_PORT` | | `depends_on:` *(commented)* | If enabled, it ensures MariaDB starts before the app | | `command:` *(commented)* | Used to wait until DB is ready before starting the app (for Node.js apps or custom logic) |
✅ **Note:** Make sure .env file contains:
APPLICATION_PORT=8080
🔹** MariaDB Service (maria_db) (Optional – Uncomment to use)**
| Key | Description | | ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | `image: mariadb:11.7.2` | Specifies the MariaDB Docker image version | | `restart: always` | Automatically restarts the database container | | `environment:` | Defines DB name, username, password, and root password using env variables | | `ports:` | Maps host DB port to container port using `DB_PORT` |
✅ **Note:** Add these in your .env file:
DB_NAME=your_db DB_USERNAME=user DB_PASSWORD=pass DB_PORT=3306
🪜 <u>**How to Enable MariaDB in Docker Compose**</u>
* Uncomment the maria_db section. * Uncomment depends_on and command in the api_service if needed. * Ensure .env file contains all required DB variables. * Run: 1. docker-compose build 2. docker-compose up
🧪<u>** Example Usage with MariaDB**</u> **Uncomment and use this block for auto-creating the database with your application:**
depends_on: ```- maria_db command: sh -c './wait-for mysql_db:3306 -- java -jar /app.jar' ```
🚀**<u> 3. Starting the Application Using Docker**</u>
✅ **Standard (Internal Config)** Use when application.yml and logback.xml are bundled inside the container:
**Step 1:** Build the application (Maven/Gradle) ```mvn clean package```
**Step 2:** Build the Docker image ```docker-compose build```
**Step 3:** Start the container ```docker-compose up```
🛠️ **<u>4. Using External application.yml</u>**
If you want to refer to an external application.yml instead of the bundled one, then follow the steps:
**Step 1:** Build the application ```mvn clean package```
**Step 2:** Build Docker image ```docker-compose build```
**Step 3:** Run the container with external config ```docker run \ -v "/absolute/path/to/application.yml:/application.yml" \ -e SPRING_CONFIG_LOCATION="file:/application.yml" \ <docker-image-name>```
⚙️ **<u>5.Using External application.yml and logback.xml</u>** If both configuration files need to be external: ```docker run \ -v "/absolute/path/to/application.yml:/application.yml" \ -v "/absolute/path/to/logback.xml:/logback.xml" \ -e SPRING_CONFIG_LOCATION="file:/application.yml" \ <docker-image-name>```
📌 <u>**Best Practices</u>** * Always provide absolute paths for mounted files.
* Keep sensitive config files out of source control.
* Use .dockerignore to exclude unnecessary files from the image build.
🧪<u> **Troubleshooting Tips</u>**
| Issue | Resolution | | --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | | `application.yml not found` | Ensure correct volume mount and entry point | | Port not accessible | Confirm `EXPOSE` matches `docker-compose` port mapping | | Logs not appearing | Check `logback.xml` path and logging configuration |

A Stop Exception (also known as CustomStopException) is typically used to halt further execution of a server-side script or request processing when certain validation fails or a critical condition is met.
**<u>Syntax:</u>** ``` <#stop> or <#stop reason> ```
Where reason: Informative message that describes the reason of the terminating error. Expression, must evaluate to a string.
**<u>Description:</u>** Aborts template processing with the given (optional) error message. This must not be used for ending template processing in normal situations! The caller of the FreeMarker template will see this as a failed template rendering, not as a normally finished one.
This directive throws a StopException, and the StopException will hold the value of the reason parameter.
**<u>Example Usage:</u>** ``` <#stop "412_Error Occured while accessing RestAPI"> ``` In Front-end UI, 412 status code is displayed and in console Error Occured while accessing RestAPI is displayed.
**<u>How to handle custom exception messages</u>**
Rather than providing an if-else condition on error handling part, JQuiver supports handling of exception messages using free marker.
Below is an example of how this can be achieved. While creating a dynamic rest api, choose FTL as the platform and in Service Logic/Package Name section, you can provide the below FTL as per the requirement.
``` <#stop "statuscode_This will contain the exception message to be rendered"> ``` Once this is done, in the error handler of ajax call, check the responseText of xhr object. This will contain your exception message provided in the "stop" command. i.e; ``` error : function(xhr, error){ showMessage(xhr.responseText, "error"); }, ``` This will display your message.

The **Activity Log** is a server-side JavaScript utility that is automatically triggered for logging user activity whenever certain actions are performed on different modules (e.g., Add, Edit, Open, Delete). It is enabled via a flag (isActivityLogEnabled = true) in the database.
* The logging is activated only when the DB column isActivityLogEnabled is set to true. * The _activityLog() function is automatically called during operations such as:
1. Creating a new form or entity 2. Editing existing data 3. Opening/viewing a form 4. Deleting a record
* The log supports capturing both default and custom parameters.
| Parameter | Description | | ------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------- | | `masterModuleType` | Entity type (e.g., dynamic-form, template, etc.) | | `typeSelect` | Indicates if the module is `system` or `custom` | | `entityName` | Name of the entity or module | | `userName` | User performing the action (e.g., `admin`, `xyz@trigyn.com`) | | `action` | Type of activity (`Add`, `Edit`, `Open`, `Delete`, etc.) | | `message` | Custom message provided by the user | | `date` | Timestamp of the action (e.g., `Fri Feb 17 11:21:58 IST 2023`) |
* Any additional parameters sent in the request are also included in the log (after filtering).
* Logs are printed directly to the server console using System.out.println().
* Useful for debugging, auditing, and monitoring user activities in real-time.
📋 **<u>Sample Activity Log (Console Output)</u>**
| **Field** | **Value** | | ---------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | `date` | Tue Jun 24 19:22:39 IST 2025 | | `isSecured` | 0 | | `isFromRestAPI` | false | | `httpRequestObject` | org.springframework.web.servlet.resource.ResourceUrlEncodingFilter\$ResourceUrlEncodingRequestWrapper\@e7bcfe9 | | `loggedInUserName` | anonymous | | `contextPath` | /demo | | `masterModuleType` | Form Builder | | `fullName` | anonymous-user | | `typeSelect` | Custom | | `loggedInUserId` | anonymous-user | | `locale` | en\_US | | `userName` | [admin@jquiver.io](mailto:admin@jquiver.io) | | `message` | *(empty)* | | `reponseTypeId` | null | | `methodTypeId` | 1 | | `viewPath` | /view | | `entityName` | test | | `apiPath` | /api | | `action` | ADD | | `loggedInUserRoleList` | \[anonymous] | | `userObject` | UserDetailsVO \[userId=anonymous-user, userName=anonymous, roleIdList=\[anonymous]] |

The Import and Export functionality allows users to upload files into the application and download files from the application for data processing, reporting, backup, and sharing purposes.
The import functionality allows users to upload supported file formats into the application for processing.
**Steps to Import Files** * Navigate to the Import section. * Click the Choose File or Browse button. * Select the required file from the local system. * Click Import or Upload. * Wait for the import process to complete.
**How Can I Configure File Storage Properties for Import and Export?**
To handle file import and export operations properly, the application requires specific file storage path configurations. These properties define where uploaded, copied, template, and EML files are stored on the server.
These configurations are maintained in the system property table and must be set correctly before using file handling features.
**Required File Storage Properties**
The following properties must be configured in the system configuration table:
| Property Name | Purpose | | ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | | `emlFileStoragePath` | Stores generated or uploaded EML files | | `file-copy-path` | Stores copied or processed files | | `file-upload-location` | Main upload directory for imported files | | `template-storage-path` | Stores template files used for import/export |
**Example Configuration**
| Property | Sample Path | | ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | | `emlFileStoragePath` | `/workspace/ARK_PHARMA/emlFileStoragePath` | | `file-copy-path` | `/workspace/ARK_PHARMA/file-copy-path` | | `file-upload-location` | `/workspace/ARK_PHARMA/file-upload-location` | | `template-storage-path` | `/workspace/ARK_PHARMA/template-storage-path` |

The Quick Jump Autocomplete feature allows users to quickly navigate to specific modules, pages, or records by typing keywords or identifiers into a search field. As the user types, relevant suggestions are dynamically displayed in a dropdown for faster access. After clicking on the displayed result in the dropdown, that particular entity is opened in a new tab.
<u>**Key Features:**</u> ✅ **Real-time Suggestions** Instantly displays matching results as the user types.
🔍 **Keyword-Based Matching** Matches page titles, IDs, names, or predefined keywords.
⚡ **Fast Navigation** Selecting a suggestion redirects the user immediately to the relevant view/page.
🧭 **Keyboard Navigation** Supports up/down arrows and Enter key for accessibility.

It is a configuration file used in Spring Boot applications to define application settings in a structured and hierarchical format using YAML syntax. It allows developers to configure environment-specific properties such as server ports, database connections, logging levels, and custom application settings in a readable and organized way. Details of which is given below in the table.
| **Property** | **Value** | **Description** | | | | ------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ | ------ | -------------------------------------- | | `server.port` | `8060` | Runs the application on port 8060 instead of default 8080 | | | | `server.shutdown` | `graceful` | Enables graceful shutdown to let in-flight requests complete | | | | `server.servlet.context-path` | `/demo` | Sets base URL path prefix for the application | | | | `server.servlet.session.persistent` | `false` | Disables persistent sessions across restarts | | | | `server.servlet.session.timeout` | `5m` | Session timeout set to 5 minutes | | | | `spring.servlet.multipart.enabled` | `true` | Enables multipart (file upload) support | | | | `spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size` | `-1` | No file size limit for uploads | | | | `spring.servlet.multipart.max-request-size` | `-1` | No total request size limit | | | | `spring.main.allow-bean-definition-overriding` | `true` | Allows overriding beans with same name | | | | `spring.web.resources.static-locations` | `classpath:/` | Serves static content from root of classpath | | | | `spring.flyway.cleanDisabled` | `true` | Disables `flyway.clean()` to prevent data loss | | | | `spring.flyway.enabled` | `true` | Enables Flyway migrations on startup | | | | `spring.datasource.driver-class-name` | `org.mariadb.jdbc.Driver` | MariaDB JDBC driver class | | | | `spring.datasource.url` | `jdbc:mariadb://localhost:3306/...` | JDBC URL for MariaDB connection with extra parameters | | | | `spring.datasource.username` | `root` | DB username (should be secured externally) | | | | `spring.datasource.password` | `root` | DB password (should be secured externally) | | | | `spring.datasource.jndi-name` | *(commented)* | JNDI config (optional, if used instead of direct JDBC) | | | | `javamelody.enabled` | `true` | Enables JavaMelody performance monitoring | | | | `javamelody.spring-monitoring-enabled` | `true` | Enables Spring bean monitoring | | | | `javamelody.init-parameters.url-exclude-pattern` | \`(/webjars/.\* | /css/.\* | ...)\` | Excludes static assets from monitoring | | `javamelody.init-parameters.monitoring-path` | `/sss` | JavaMelody UI available at `/demo/sss` | | | | `logging.config` | `classpath:logback.xml` | Uses custom logback config | | | | `logging.level.root` | `info` | Global log level set to `info` | | | | `logging.level.org.flywaydb` | `info` | Flyway-specific log level set to `info` | | |

The Tags module enables users to label and manage specific versions of code configurations. It provides a structured mechanism to track changes and revert to previously saved states, ensuring controlled version management within the application.
**<u>Follow the steps below to configure a new Tag Model.</u>**
**Step 1:** Navigate to the Tags button from the JQuiver home page for redirecting to the tags module.
The Tags module provides a centralized interface to access and manage tags across all modules such as Templating, REST API,Files and others. It allows users to select entities and create tags to mark specific code states for easy tracking and reversion.
![](/cf/files/f2b36f99-92dc-47b1-8c48-c8b8d6c56aad)
**Step 2:** Select the required module and entity to enable tag creation. * Use the Entity Type dropdown to choose the desired module (e.g., Templating, REST API, Files, etc.). * Use the Entity Name field to search and locate the specific entity. * Select the checkbox corresponding to the required entity. * Once an entity is selected, the Create Tag button becomes enabled for further action.
![](/cf/files/139465bd-16d0-4e06-bbcc-32636ff0ab27)
**Step 3:**Create a new tag for the selected entity.
* Click on the Create Tag button once it is enabled. * The Create Tag popup will be displayed. * Enter the required Tag Name to identify the code version. * Click Save to create the tag temporarily. * Click Save & Return on the main page to persist the tag permanently in the system.
![](/cf/files/579f28cc-6f1e-4c3c-9f85-ff88b3be5d46)
**Step 4:** Access the created tags from the View Tag button from Tags Module.
**Step 5:** View, search, filter, and manage configured tag modules. * The left panel displays all available tag modules. * Use the Search field in the left panel to quickly locate tag modules. * Use the Show dropdown option to filter modules based on categories such as Favourites or All Modules. * Selecting a tag module displays the linked modules and entities in the right-side listing section. * The listing contains Module Name, Entity Name, and available Actions.
The following actions are available for each tagged module:
* Edit – Allows modification of the selected tag details. * Delete – Removes the selected tag from the system. * Restore Tag – Restores the code/configuration to the selected tag version.
Click Back to return to the previous screen or navigation menu.
![](/cf/files/6112649c-47bb-43b7-94f0-25db7edffc79)
**Step 6:** Manage linked modules using the available actions.
The following actions are available for each linked module:
* View – Opens the linked module configuration. * Remove Tag – Removes the association between the module and the tag module. * History – Displays the code history details of the linked module for tracking previous changes.
![](/cf/files/6112649c-47bb-43b7-94f0-25db7edffc79)
